question_title: What will be the value of "4-Week Treasury Bill Secondary Market Rate, Discount Basis" on 2026-05-13?
summary: The 4-Week Treasury Bill Secondary Market Rate (DTB4WK) is forecast to be approximately 3.62% on May 13, 2026. The Fed held rates steady at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, with no FOMC meeting expected before resolution. The rate has been in an extremely stable regime (3.57-3.63) since early April with a standard deviation of only 0.017%. Monte Carlo simulations using O-U mean-reverting and random-walk models, starting from an adjusted 3.62% (accounting for Trading Economics May 5 data showing a slight uptick), produce a tight distribution centered at 3.62 with an 80% CI of [3.57, 3.67]. The distribution is slightly wider than pure regime statistics would suggest, allowing for tail risks from unexpected macro shocks, but the lack of a scheduled FOMC meeting and stable monetary policy stance constrain the range tightly.

factors.description: Fed held rates at 3.50-3.75% on Apr 29, no FOMC meeting before resolution - pins short-term rates near current levels
DTB4WK in extremely tight range (3.57-3.63) for 20 trading days with regime std of only 0.0166 - very low volatility regime
Trading Economics shows 4-week yield at 3.66% on May 5 (yield basis ~2-3bp higher than discount basis) suggesting FRED value may be ~3.63-3.64 already
Rate futures imply gradual increases to 3.62-3.70 by Oct-Jan 2027 - slight upward bias but negligible over 6-8 business days
4 FOMC dissents (most since 1992) and geopolitical tensions create tail risk of unexpected rate movements

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: DTB4WK on 2026-05-13**

**Median estimate: 3.62%** (80% CI: 3.57 - 3.67%)

Key reasoning:
- **Stable regime**: The 4-week T-bill rate has been locked in a tight 3.57-3.63% range for the past month with remarkably low volatility (σ = 0.017%)
- **Fed policy anchor**: FOMC held rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29; no meeting expected before May 13. This effectively pins short-term rates
- **Slight upward adjustment**: Trading Economics data suggests the rate may have ticked up slightly to ~3.63-3.64% by May 5, so I centered the distribution at 3.62 rather than the FRED-confirmed 3.61
- **Tight distribution**: With no policy catalyst on the horizon, extreme moves are very unlikely. My 90% CI spans only 12 basis points (3.56-3.68)
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What will be the value of "4-Week Treasury Bill Secondary Market Rate, Discount Basis" on 2026-05-13?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43406)
[4-Week Treasury Bill Secondary Market Rate, Discount Basis (DTB4WK) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DTB4WK)
[3-Month Treasury Bill Secondary Market Rate, Discount Basis (DTB3) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DTB3)
resolution_criteria: Resolves to the value found on the FRED API for the series DTB4WK once the data is published.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will paginate through the data found at the API endpoint `https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=DTB4WK`. This endpoint includes the latest data for the series. The latest revised data will be used when the resolution is assessed. The datapoint matching 2026-05-13 will be used to determine the resolution of this question.

A datapoint matches if:
1. The series is updated daily and the date of the datapoint is within 1 day previous to the resolution date.
2. The series is updated weekly and the date of the datapoint is within 7 days previous to the resolution date.
3. The series is updated monthly and the date of the datapoint is within 31 days previous to the resolution date.

If the datapoint is clearly skipped, or no datapoint is found after 2 weeks of checking then the question will be annulled.

factors.description: Fed just held rates at 3.50-3.75% on Apr 29 with no change expected until June FOMC meeting - pins short-term rates near current levels
DTB4WK has been in extremely tight range (3.57-3.63) for entire month with regime std of only 0.0166 - very low volatility
Latest value is 3.61, regime mean is 3.5995 - current value is essentially at the mean
Rate futures imply gradual increases to 3.62-3.70 by Oct-Jan 2027, slight upward pressure over months but negligible over 8 days
Tail risk: market shock (flight-to-quality or inflation scare) could move rates significantly, but very unlikely in 8 business days

input.anchor: Regime mean of 3.5995% from 20 observations in stable regime since April 6, 2026 (FRED DTB4WK series)
input.assessment: The 4-week T-bill rate is effectively pinned by the federal funds rate target of 3.50-3.75%. The FOMC just met on April 29 and held rates steady, with the next meeting likely in June. Over the 8 business days until resolution (May 13), there is no scheduled policy event that could materially shift this rate. The rate has been remarkably stable, ranging only from 3.57 to 3.63 over the past 20 trading days with a standard deviation of just 0.017%. My Monte Carlo simulations (normal random walk, bootstrap, and O-U mean-reverting models) all converge on a median around 3.60 with an 80% CI of roughly 3.54-3.66. The blended simulation gives P10=3.54, P50=3.60, P90=3.66.
input.calibration_notes: Very low divergence from anchor. The rate is nearly constant. Main calibration concern is whether tails are wide enough for an unexpected shock, but with no FOMC meeting and stable macro backdrop, tails beyond ±0.10 from current are already quite conservative. The question bounds (3.0175-4.9396) are extremely wide relative to plausible outcomes.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Unexpected macro shock (geopolitical crisis, tariff escalation) that could move rates; 2. Treasury auction dynamics that could temporarily shift 4-week bill supply/demand; 3. Whether the 4 FOMC dissents signal imminent policy shift (unlikely in 8 days)
input.tool_audit: FRED data via web_search: excellent, returned full observation history and regime stats. Fed statement via fetch_url: very useful, confirmed rate hold. Monte Carlo simulations: ran 3 models and blended. All tools performed well with no failures.
input.update_triggers: Emergency FOMC meeting, major geopolitical crisis, sudden market liquidity event, unexpected economic data release
input.process_reflection: Straightforward question with abundant data. The FRED API integration in web_search was excellent - returned structured data including regime stats and rate futures in a single call. The sandbox for Monte Carlo worked smoothly. No friction points for this question type.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Factual Claims Check

**Factor 1** ("Fed just held rates at 3.50-3.75% on Apr 29"): Confirmed. The trace shows the FOMC statement fetch returned: "maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent." The vote was 8-4. ✓

**Factor 2** ("DTB4WK has been in extremely tight range 3.57-3.63 for entire month with regime std of only 0.0166"): Confirmed. The FRED API structured data returned `"stable_since": "2026-04-06", "stable_mean": 3.5995, "stable_std": 0.016576, "observations_in_regime": 20` and the observations array shows values from 3.57 to 3.63. ✓

**Factor 3** ("Latest value is 3.61, regime mean is 3.5995"): Confirmed. FRED data shows `"latest_value": 3.61, "latest_date": "2026-05-01"`. ✓

**Factor 4** ("Rate futures imply gradual increases to 3.62-3.70 by Oct-Jan 2027"): Confirmed. The FRED structured data includes rate_futures: Oct 2026 = 3.62, Nov = 3.65, Dec = 3.68, Jan 2027 = 3.70. ✓

**Factor 5** (Tail risk): Judgment-based factor, no factual verification needed. ✓

### Double-Counting Check

Factors 1 and 2 are closely related — the tight range in Factor 2 exists precisely BECAUSE the Fed held rates steady (Factor 1). However, they represent distinct informational angles (policy anchor vs. statistical stability), which is standard practice for numeric forecasting.

### Resolution Criteria Alignment

The resolution criteria specify the FRED DTB4WK value for 2026-05-13. The agent correctly targets this. ✓

### Potential Issue: Stale Starting Point

The most significant concern is the Trading Economics snippet in the agent's own web search results: "The yield on US 4 Week Bill Yield eased to 3.66% on May 5, 2026." This is the day-of-forecast market rate, 5 basis points above the FRED latest of 3.61% (May 1). The agent acknowledged this in its counterargument ("Trading Economics showing 4-week yield already at 3.66% on May 5 - this is at the edge of my distribution already") but did NOT adjust its center estimate or starting point for the Monte Carlo simulation.

HOWEVER, this is mitigated by:
1. Trading Economics reports "over-the-counter interbank yield quotes" which differs from FRED's "discount basis" rate — yield equivalent is always slightly higher than discount basis.
2. The 3.66% figure could represent an intraday quote on May 5 that may not persist to the FRED close.
3. The FRED series hasn't published the May 2-5 data yet in the trace.

Still, even accounting for the yield-vs-discount basis difference (typically ~1-2bp for a 4-week bill), the market is clearly higher than the April average. The agent's center of 3.60 may be slightly low — a center of ~3.62-3.63 might better reflect the May 5 starting point.

### Worldview Consistency

The archived worldview research on Fed policy (from April 15) states "There is no FOMC meeting in April 2026" — which was incorrect, as the April 28-29 meeting actually occurred. However, the agent's current trace correctly identified and used the April 29 meeting results, so the stale worldview entry didn't cause an error.

### Tool Audit

Agent claims "All tools performed well with no failures." Ground truth confirms 13 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). No discrepancy. ✓

### Counterargument Quality

The counterargument is substantive — it identifies the 4 FOMC dissents, incoming data risks, Treasury auction dynamics, geopolitical risks, and notably flags the Trading Economics 3.66% figure. This is a strong counterargument. ✓

### Confidence Assessment

0.72 is reasonable given the high data quality and stable regime, offset by the 8-day horizon uncertainty and the Trading Economics signal suggesting the rate may have moved higher. ✓

### Probability Assessment

The agent's center of 3.60 with 80% CI of 3.54-3.66 is supported by the Monte Carlo simulations (all three models converge on median ~3.60). However, the Trading Economics May 5 data suggesting the rate has already moved to ~3.66 (even accounting for yield vs discount basis, this implies FRED DTB4WK may already be around 3.63-3.64) argues for a slightly higher center (~3.62-3.63). This is a modest concern — the difference is within the distribution but the center may be ~2-3bp too low given the most recent market data.

This doesn't rise to a **fail** because: (1) the yield-vs-discount basis distinction means the actual FRED value is likely lower than 3.66, (2) the agent's P90 of 3.66 does capture this scenario, and (3) the difference between 3.60 and 3.62-3.63 as center is within reasonable uncertainty bounds. But it warrants a **warn** because the agent acknowledged evidence that its starting point may be stale and didn't adjust.
